labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: Some opens about the avtcore-frame-acknowledgement draft proposal
issues.author: taste1981
issues.body:
Hi,

I read the avtcore-frame-acknowledgement document, and I have a few questions:

1.	If the request for a frame is sent, and receiver has already decoded corresponding frame, but subsequent decoding error happens and receiver decides to flush the decode picture buffers(DPB) before it acks the request. Should receiver still send the ACK?

2.	For uniquely identifying a frame, should we always rely on existence of DD for this? Or we should rely on some more generic frame markers? Or this can be from application

3.	Any consideration why we do not use timestamp instead?

4.	In the context of SFU/MCU, where you have 1 sender and multiple receivers, how would you expect those feedbacks to be routed and handled by sender?

Regards
Johnny

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
1. I think it should respond, though in practice it won't really matter. If a receiver has a failure of that type, it will need to send a keyframe request to recover anyway.
2.  The idea is that we have a prioritized list of identification methods, and we pick the first available one. DD is relatively straightforward so I added that as a startpoint. I'll open a separate issue on this topic, because I'm starting to doubt it's the right choice.
3. Timestamps alone are not enough, it would need to be at least timestamp + spatial index (which might not be easily available). With the timestamp scale being 90kHz we'd also need a rather large data type to avoid potential wraparound issues.
4. For and SFU/SFM I don't really see an option other than to ack only those frames acked by _all_ receivers for which that frame has been forwarded. For an MCU however, the uplink can be considered a separate point-to-point connection and then it's kind up to the encode/send part of the server to be designed in a way that makes sense for its application. The receivers will just blindly ack anything they decode.

issues.title: Add a better definition of what a "frame" is
issues.author: sprangerik
issues.body:
The current draft says that what is being acknowledged is that "video frames have been received and decoded", but we should probably be more precise what we mean with a frame.

For the context of this message, what we are interested in is that the sender can reason about the codec state. A potential phrasing could be the we acknowledge:

> ...any decodable unit of bitstream data that results in the update of codec state (e.g. reference buffers, entropy tables, etc) that can be used as a reference for any subsequent decodable unit of bitstream data.

When using SVC with inter-layer dependencies (i.e. references between e.g. spatial or quality layers), then each layer would constitute a separate frame. That seems relatively straightforward.

However, several codecs support _segmentation_ where a frame is split into several independent segments in order to better utilize pipelining or parallelism. If such a segment supports referencing state updates from a different segment within the same frame - then in this case each segment could in fact be acked independently. This would complicate the frame id mechanism as well.

issues.title: Consider supporting acknowledgement of received but not decoded frames
issues.author: sprangerik
issues.body:
The current text talks only about acking frames that have been received with a guarantee that the receiver will _attempt_ to decode it.

A natural question is if there is value in also acknowledging that a frames has been received - but might _not_ be used for decoding (e.g. non-base layer temporal frames).

Since this message is primarily designed to allow a sender to reason about the decoder state, this doesn't at first glance seem that useful. But it would be good to know if someone has a valid use, or else just document why this is _not_ included.

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body:
Can we consider extending status field to indicate 
0 = Not received (may be split partially received, fully lost frame)
1 = Received, not decoded (completely assembled and submitted to decode)
2 = decoded 
3 = decode error
In case of decode error, PLI request would be sent but sender does not understand why PLI was issued. Decode error info is useful for sender to help adapt for certain clients/decoder.
I feel more info sender has it can act better.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
I'm not really against it, but I'm trying to build a mental model of how it would actually be used/implemented.

Do we need to distinguish between decoder errors that are "recoverable" or not? E.g. consider the cases where:
1. A decoder returns an error code because the bitstream data is malformed and decoding of that frame could be completed. No internal state is updated and the decoder is still "alive" in the same state as before the decode call was made.
2. A (presumably HW) decoder suffers some form of failure (e.g. OOM) and the entire decoder context is torn down and deallocated.

The first could be recovered from with a feedback. The second would necessitate a full keyframe.

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body: I believe decode errors triggers PLI requests that would make issuing IDR frame. Having decode status in feedback helps sender take actions if LTR frame decode was successful or triggered error. Sender is aware of frames sent so that it can correct in case there are any faulty decoder exists. At this point I am not sure we need to distinguish decode errors. If there are more cases to be covered, dont see problem expanding decode error status.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
> I believe decode errors triggers PLI requests that would make issuing IDR frame.

Ifaik, PLI isn't explicitly intended as a keyframe request (even if that is how libwebrtc currently uses it). Rather it's what is call, a "picture loss indication" and it should be up to the receiver to deal with that loss. FIR is strictly a keyframe request however.

I'm still not entirely convinced there are realistic cases where all of the following are true:

1. The jitter buffer logic determines frame A is decodable
2. The decoder despite that fails to decode frame A
3. The decoder is still in a usable state and _can_ successfully decode a new frame, using previous state as reference.

In my experience, decode errors are very rare - and once they occur the decoder is essentially in an unknown state and needs to be reset anyway.

Do you have any data to the contrary?

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body:
Do not have data to show, agree that decode errors are very rare for atleast older codec formats (since decoders have matured).
Keeping the status helps future cases, based on that suggestion was made. Alternatively, option to expand new status would help add such cases Ex: reserved status etc.
While experimenting feature, it can be defined if needed.
Wdyt?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body: That sounds good to me, let's keep 3 as "reserved" for now and we can decide later if that is the right use case.

issues.title: Consider adding explicit frame id to the Frame Acknowledgement Request header
issues.author: sprangerik
issues.body:
The current text does not specify how a frame should be identified - instead there is a prioritized list of alternatives for identifying frames, and the first available one on the list should be used. There are some pros and cons to this:

Pros
* Avoids redundant frame id if it's already part of the transmission
* Makes the request header itself very simple

Cons
* Risk that the mechanism is fragile (e.g. if sender/receiver has different views of which id is in fact being used). Though this could be mitigated with an additional SDP attribute.
* Some id mechanism requires tuples (e.g. timestamp + spatial layer, or even subindexing into segments).
* Some mechanism have data in the payload, which might not be available to an SFU/SFM.
* Some mechanism have large gaps between frames (e.g. using 90kHz clock timestamps), necessitating larger than desired sizes of the id fields in order to avoid wraparound issues.
* A sender might only be interested in a small subset of frames, an generic id mechanisms will typically identify all frames - requiring status to be transmitted for "uninteresting" frames as well, in case a span of more than one frame is being inquired about.

To avoid the quite large list of cons, it might be desirable to explicitly identify the frames as part of the Frame Acknowledgement Request header extension instead. It should solve all of the cons with a relatively low overhead of extra data and slightly more complex parsing of the header.

comments.author: alfredh
comments.body:
Another way to encode it could be to include number of frames to report in the header,
for example: uint8_t count

The base frame id could be the one from DD header, and count could be number of frames before that.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body: I'm not sure I like the offset counter for the frame id itself, but a count would indeed feel nicer than the absolute "to" I mention in https://github.com/sprangerik/frame-acknowledgement/issues/5

issues.title: Consider add a "to" field in the request header
issues.author: sprangerik
issues.body:
The current text specified that the Frame Acknowledgement Request has the property that it "...implies a status request for all frames starting at the given frame ID, up to and including the frame contained in the RTP packet the header extension is attached to...".

The sender might in fact not be interested in all those frames - it might just be attempting to request a retransmit of the status for a single frame. For such use cases it would be useful with both a "start" and a "end" frame id, or potentially some type of flag that identified the id as both start and end if it's a single id.

This might complicate and enlarge the header extension, but also makes it more clear since there is no longer any ambiguity in what the end should be (if the packet it is attached to is not a frame).

issues.title: SDP to negotiate RTP extension
issues.author: shrim27
issues.body: Should RFC include SDP syntax for defining SDP extension header ID. Probably RFC URL can be used to define extension type.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body: Yes, that's a good point. We should also add SDP considerations for the RTCP message.

issues.title: Frame acknowledgment request on recovered packets
issues.author: shrim27
issues.body:
Does the draft need to explain how acknowledgment request on recovered packet be addressed. Ex: Nack or FEC recovered packet might have frame acknowledgement request, does receiver need to handle them same way as original request or neglect them.
I think considering them as original probably is better way, not sure you have any considerations.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
Good point, this should be clearly specified.

I think a reasonable action would be to treat them as original - unless a later feedback request has already been processed, in which case it should be ignored. The same point should hold for any case where there is packet reordering.

issues.title: Receiver triggered Frame acknowledgement
issues.author: shrim27
issues.body:
Current format is using request-based approach, keeping receiver dumb as much as possible. There is a advantage in having receiver triggered feedback, sender request could be overhead which may not be necessary for certain applications. 
SDP format can be added to distinguish between different modes such that application developer can choose what fits best. 
Feedback mainly helps under packet loss, and providing configuration like trigger feedback if no fresh frame is submitted to decoder for 'x' timeout time and retrigger it every new frame received but cant be submitted to decode since packet loss has not resolved. These help receiver recover faster rather than wait for new feedback request from sender.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
That might be interesting, although I feel like packet-level feedback (e.g. TWCC or RFC8888) might already be enough.

If we were to support receive-side initiated feedback, it could interfere with the mechanism which describes which frames states the receiver needs to keep track of. It might be that in this case we need mark the feedback as being out of bounds with respect to the requested feedback.

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body:
Problem with using (TWCC or RFC 8888) is they dont provide current frame serviced by decoder hence it not very useful for LTR during packet loss.
For cases where RTD is high, sender requested feedback is very delayed and can lead to big impacts on decoder recovery time. Hence proposing having mode where receiver can trigger the feedback.
We could negotiate mode using SDP also RTCP message can have flag to indicate sender requested or receiver generated to distinguish, so that there is less for interpretation.
In terms of frames to keep track of I was suggesting interval can be defined, (Ex:  similar to "rtx-time=" ) shared details in another thread. Feedback could include highest frameID decoded to highest frame-ID received.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
The request shouldn't be delayed. If the sender knows it wants feedback for a given frame N (e.g. because it has been assigned as an LTR frame), the sender will add the request header extension to the very packets containing the payload data for frame N - so that the receiver can send feedback as soon as the frame has been assembled.

If we add a status symbol for "frame partially received", that could be sent immediately to notify the sender so that it can determine which recovery method to use for recovery (e.g. FEC/RTX/LTR/etc)

Maybe it would help if we had concrete examples.

wdyt?

issues.title: Mechanism to decode LTR frame
issues.author: shrim27
issues.body:
Not sure this is relevant for draft but in general how do you think frame assembly would work with frame encoded using LTR frame.
As per my understanding webrtc currently waits for packet loss to recover or IDR frame to arrive for unblocking frames received to start decoding.
With frame acknowledgment, no longer need to wait for IDR, do you think webrtc frame assembly need to change and what information is needed for it handle this new feature? I think DD extension has mechanisms defined which could be useful here. Checking if there is any thoughts on this.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
I don't think the receiver logic needs any update. At least in libwebrtc, the video jitter buffer looks at the dependency chain and waits for frames to be decodable - it doesn't really distinguish between recovery via retransmissions, fec, or new frames with dependencies on received frames.

We also don't really care if the dependencies are described via DependecyDescriptors, FrameMarking or one of the older payload-specific formats.

In essence, recover via LTR isn't different from say temporal layering and fast-forwarding to the next TL0 frame if one or more upper-layer frames have become stalled.

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body: Thanks Erik, if you could point to libwebrtc code where its handled would be helpful.

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
> Thanks Erik, if you could point to libwebrtc code where its handled would be helpful.

The code is spread out across a number of classes, but in general is coordinated by this one: https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:third_party/webrtc/video/rtp_video_stream_receiver2.h

issues.title: Consider if we need a way to signal "not interest in feedback for frames prior to X"
issues.author: sprangerik
issues.body:
The current proposal specifies that "If a new Frame Acknowledgement Request is sent with an incremented Frame ID, all status values prior to that Frame ID are considered as acknowledged and can be culled by the receiver. ".

However, a situation could feasibly arise where a sender does not request feedback for an extended period of time simply because it is not interested in feedback at that time. The receiver cannot know that and must thus keep buffering frame states, possibly indefinitely.

A workaround is to require the sender to request feedback with some interval, and it would then simply ignore the feedback.

It would be nicer if we had a way to signal that the sender is not interested in feedback prior to frame X, all such state can be culled, and no immediate feedback is requested.

comments.author: shrim27
comments.body: Something similar to "rtx-time=<rtx-time-val>" defined rfc4588 could be used as reference here.

issues.title: Motivation for requesting feedback about more than just current frame in Frame Acknowledgment Request
issues.author: gchandok
issues.body:
The current draft says "The request implies a status request for all frames starting at the given frame ID, up to and including the frame contained in the RTP packet the header extension is attached to - even if that frame is not yet complete."

Can you expand on the use case for this? Why is it not sufficient to just ask for feedback about the frame contained in the RTP packet the header extension is attached to?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body: I think this issues is a dupe of issue #14. It's to be able to essentially make a retransmission request for feedback of a frame if we think it won't arrive in a timely manner.

comments.author: gchandok
comments.body: Thanks that helped. There were two parts to my question I do have the answer for one "why can't we just imply current frame". The second part is why do we need solicit feedback for all frames upto current (i.e a range) vs requesting feedback for 1 frame. Do we need to confirm the decode of more than just the most recent LTR frame?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
Yes, there are a few cases where independent acknowledgements are useful.

One of them is when sending a simulcast-like mode on a single SSRC (e.g. using an [S3T1](https://www.w3.org/TR/webrtc-svc/#S3T1*) scalability mode). There the most recent fram per spatial layer is required.

Another case occurs when we consider multi-way applications. If on receiver has decoded frames {1, 3, 4} and another decoded {2, 3, 5} - then actually 3 would be signalled back to the sender as the only acked frame even if the receivers individually at at 4 and 5 respectively.

issues.title: Use case for sending frame acknowledgement request on an RTP packet not containing a video frame
issues.author: gchandok
issues.body:
The current draft says, "If the extension is attached to a packet not containing a video frame, the feedback should be up to and including the immediately preceding frame ID."

What is the use case for sending frame acknowledgement request on an RTP packet not containing a video frame?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
The use-case is to enable guaranteed delivery of feedback. E.g. if either feedback request or the RTCP feedback response is lost, the sender can just request feedback covering the same range again.

This can be done either reactively (reacting to e.g. packet loss signals or timeouts) or preemptively (e.g. always sending two requests per frame with some spacing in order to reduce risk of lost information).

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body: ...and just to clarify, if you want to re-send a feedback request X ms later, you might not have a frame ready to be sent. In that case you can attach the feedback request to any other packet on the same SSRC, such as a padding packet.

comments.author: gchandok
comments.body:
Sounds good. Can we document this in the draft? 

By padding packets I am guessing you are referring to https://webrtc.googlesource.com/src/+/HEAD/pc/g3doc/rtp.md#padding-probes? What RTP timestamps do you use for such packets?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
Sure, adding that to the doc would be good. Let me do that in a separate PR so the latest state can make progress on the latest state.

Padding probes are the most common padding packet sent today, yes. I think the timestamp will be the same as the last sent frame. The libwebrtc source that sets timestamps for packets should be this: https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:third_party/webrtc/modules/rtp_rtcp/source/packet_sequencer.cc

issues.title: Define an RTP header extension for the frame acknowledgement for cases when there is a return traffic
issues.author: gchandok
issues.body: As described in https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9392.pdf, we can avoid the overhead of sending RTCP packets when there is a return channel (e.g. in case of a 2 way video call). For those cases can we define an RTP header extension to carry the same acknowledgment information as the RTCP message?

comments.author: sprangerik
comments.body:
I think that technically we can, but then we couldn't use that if there is no return channel. For things like remote desktop and cloud gaming there typically won't be - so there we'd need the RTCP message anyway.
I'm not sure it's worth the complexity to add both a header extension and an RTCP message, especially if we'll need seemingly fragile logic to map incoming header extension on incoming media to some other state on outgoing media on a different SSRC...

pulls.title: Improve definition of a frame
pulls.author: sprangerik
pulls.body:
Adds a more unambiguous definition of a frame for the context of this document.

Fixes #2

comments.body: Is slice be treated as frame since it is decodable unit and can result in codec state update (entropy table)
...makes updates `to` encoder state...?

That fits the definition yes. As long as it meets this definition and the other requirements as well (e.g. independently addressable with a frame id) that I don't see why you couldn't use it that way. Not sure of the use case, but it should be technically possible.

Updated draft to explicitly mention slice.

I would have liked frame_ID be tied to complete frame since LTR or any other feature would work for complete frame. But not against having this, since it provides unique ID to slice which could be helpful for some use-cases.

You can absolutely still be done on a full-frame basis if that's what you want to do. Just put the FrameID on the last packet of a frame (i.e. the RTP packet with marker bit set for that frame), that would implicitly cover the whole frame. 
This new definition just makes it possible to mark parts of a frame with FrameIDs in order to make other uses cases possible _in addition to_ what we already have. You don't need to mark a sub-frame with a FrameID unless you are interested in that specific part being decoded for purposes of how you encode subsequent sub-frames. That's a very niche use case.
Seems fine to me to have this granularity if needed

pulls.title: Add both Frame ID and Frame Feedback Request to the same extensions
pulls.author: sprangerik
pulls.body:
This PR removes usage of an external identification mechanism (such as Dependency Descriptors) in favor of having it built-in to the same extensions. 

This makes the logic much cleaner and unambiguous.
Since we now only deal with frames the sender is interested in feedback about, we can reduce the ID to a single 8-bit value. "Being interested in" implies that a reference frame state is being kept in anticipation of being re-used, so up to 256 outstanding frames should be plenty.

comments.body: Can you please expand what FFR stands for.
Frame ID - 8 bits seems very small, for 120fps or higher wrap arounds very quickly (less than 2seconds). I think 16bits should be better.
Can you add ascii reference to 2 byte header as well?

What is the usecase for this? Is this request sent on non-video packets? Frame-ID probably needed for all packets, making that optional seems not required. Please help understand if you have something particular case in mind.
It's intended to mean "Frame id / Feedback Request" iirc. Will update.

I think it's actually plenty since we now only put IDs on frames which we are interested in, meaning that we keep encoder state alive pending feedback.

Consider the case where each new frame is stored in a cyclic buffer and signaled for feedback. If the fps is 120 and the RTT is 66ms, then you'll already have 8 frames in flight at any given moment - mean you'll need at least 12 reference buffers. That's already exhausting the total available AV1 reference buffers, other codecs  might have even fewer available. So in order to make that kind of scheme tenable you really need to spread the available reference buffers out over time and only persistently store a subset of them. 

My point is that the size of frame ID space should be viewed in the context of the maximum number of reference buffers available, which in the current major codecs seems to be anywhere from 3 to 16.

That's intended mostly for resilience, e.g. retransmission of frame feedback or just preemptively sending redundant feedback requests.

As long the packet is on the same media SSRC I don't think we need to restrict the header to be on video packets. E.g. sending it on an RTX or padding packet should be fine, as long as you don't try to add a Frame ID there.
Sure, I'll add that.

My main concern is with varied network and fps use-cases wrap around should not create issue with incorrect mapping of frame_ID to sender frame number. To me 256 seems very small range which could create issues interpreting the feedback on sender side.

I disagree, if you have more than 32 _marked_ frames in flight you are already on very thin ice. The way to solve that is to just not stash new frames (or at least reduce the rate of them) until you receive feedback for the pending data.
I guess that should say "retransmission", not RTX - as the latter will typically use a different SSRC.

We should add considerations about the SFU case where a recevier may be switched over to a different stream without the chance to provide feedback for some frames then it could provide a stale response when it switches back to the stream, by which time the frameID may have rolled over. We should provide some guidance on culling all decoder state when the stream swiches on the receiver. Also the SFU should consider the any pending feedback requests acknowledge post a stream switch
Above text is standard for all header extensions. Just referring RFC5285 should sufficient
What is the use can for 00? We are tagging a frame as long term reference but not asking for feedback for it?

In a separate issue you mentioned we may want to re-request feedback on a padding packet. Have you considered adding an explicit RTCP message for this? Sending a padding RTP packet for this seems hacky.

> In a separate issue you mentioned we may want to re-request feedback on a padding packet. Have you considered adding an explicit RTCP message for this? Sending a padding RTP packet for this seems hacky.

For the vast majority of cases, sending requests in a padding packet wont be necessary. You would typically only have these headers on actual video frames. Having it on a padding packet is indeed a bit hacky, but I don't think we need to prevent that usage either.

I did consider using an RTCP message, but decided not to for a few reasons. Primarily it's that it would necessitate another packet - and apart from extra overhead that might lead to those packets having different timing and the possibility of loss of just on of the two. We'd also and up with complication of an RTP header extension plus two RTCP messages...

> We should add considerations about the SFU case where a recevier may be switched over to a different stream without the chance to provide feedback for some frames then it could provide a stale response when it switches back to the stream, by which time the frameID may have rolled over. We should provide some guidance on culling all decoder state when the stream swiches on the receiver. Also the SFU should consider the any pending feedback requests acknowledge post a stream switch

That's a very good point. I'll add a section about that. I think the whole sequence number handling requires a paragraph...

My thinking there was around the (yet to be defined) receive-side triggered feedback mode.
But it's a fair point. I'll remove it / mark it as reserved for now - and then let's iterate on that use case.
Done.
Added a "Frame ID consideration" section. ptal!
We can discuss the explicit RTCP message separately

"the sender MUST increment the Frame ID by one for each new frame in sending order"

should we change each new frame, to each new "long term reference frame". Something to indicate that this is not expected to increase for each frame just frames considered reference frames by the encoder. Frame ID won't be present for every frame but reading this sentence in isolation causes some confusion

When considering a multi-way application with an SFU/SFM-type relay in the middle, the middlebox may need to do translations/rewriting of Frame IDs such that the outgoing FrameIDs **from** a middlebox to a receiver still fulfill**~~s~~** the requirement that the FrameIDs are incremented by one for each new frame that is marked for feedback. This must be true even if independent video streams for different senders are multiplexed onto the same SSRC. Further the middlebox should typically not acknowledge a frame to a sender unless all active receivers have acknowledged that frame.
@sprangerik I think we need some more info in Frame ID considerations to cover the SFU implications above. We can do that in a separate PR.
